content.text: Input / Output Design

How you shape a tool's inputs and outputs determines whether the model can use it accurately and efficiently. Inputs define what the model must provide; outputs define what the model sees when deciding its next step. Both directly affect token consumption, error rates, and the number of API round-trips needed per task.

attrs.alt: Input and output design principles for tools
attrs.title: Tool Input/Output Design Principles

content.text: Every byte a tool returns is injected into the next API call as input tokens. The model only needs enough to make its next decision — return the minimum that serves that purpose.
Input Design: Minimal Required Parameters

Only ask for parameters the tool actually uses. Every required field the model must fill is an opportunity for a wrong value. If a parameter can be defaulted, make it optional. If it can be derived from context (current user, timestamp), inject it in the handler.
Input Design: Strong Types and Constraints
JSON Schema supports
minimum
maximum
maxLength
pattern
, and
enum
. These both guide the model toward valid values and let you reject bad inputs early.
Output Design: Return What the Model Needs Next

Ask yourself: what does the model need to make its next decision? Return exactly that. For list operations, return IDs and summaries — not full records. Reserve full details for a follow-up get-by-id call.
Output Design: Structured Errors

Tool failures are part of normal operation. Return structured error dicts with a machine-readable code and a human-readable message. This lets the model decide intelligently whether to retry, try a different tool, or inform the user.
Output Design: Pagination Tokens
For any operation that can return large result sets, include a
next_page_token
in the output. This lets the model request subsequent pages by passing the token back as an input parameter — no need to re-specify the original query.
Trimming Oversized Outputs

When a tool calls an external API that returns a large payload — a full HTML page, a lengthy JSON document, a raw search result — trim it before returning. Convert it to the smallest representation the model needs.
Summary
Keep required parameters to a minimum — inject context (user ID, timestamp, API version) in the handler, not via the model.
Use JSON Schema constraints (
enum
minimum
maxLength
pattern
) to guide the model and validate early.
Return only what the model needs next — IDs and summaries for lists, full details only on explicit follow-up.
Return structured errors with a code, message, and
retryable
flag — never raw exceptions or stack traces.
Add
next_page_token
and
has_more
to list operations so the model can paginate without re-specifying query parameters.
Trim large external payloads (HTML, JSON blobs) before returning — verbose outputs waste tokens and slow the agent.
